title: The Computational Horizon: Benchmarking AI-Driven Retrosynthesis Against a Topological Complexity Gradient.
abstract: While Computer-Aided Synthesis Planning (CASP) excels at standard benchmarks, its operational limits regarding topological complexity remain undefined. This study evaluates three leading architectures Transformer-based (IBM RXN), Graph-based (ASKCOS), and Template-based (AiZynthFinder) against a curated "Complexity Gradient" of natural products ranging from industrial standards to supramolecular giants. Our results identify a deterministic "Complexity Cliff" at a Bertz Complexity Score of approximately 2,000. Below this threshold (e.g., Thymol), models achieved convergence. However, performance collapsed for stereochemically dense targets. Notably, template-based systems exhibited "Template Hallucination," generating high-confidence but chemically invalid routes for complex targets like Taxol. At the supramolecular limit (Maitotoxin, CT > 8000), all 2 architectures hit a "Zero-Shot Wall." We conclude that current AI paradigms excel at tactical pattern retrieval within a narrow window but lack the stereoelectronic reasoning required for complex de novo synthesis, necessitating a "Human-in-the-Loop" workflow.
